The *Strahlenschutzverordnung* (StrlSchV) is the German regulation titled *Verordnung zum Schutz vor der schädlichen Wirkung ionisierender Strahlung*. It serves as the implementing legislation for the protection against the harmful effects of ionizing radiation, updating previous directives to align with the EU Directive 2013/59/Euratom [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:ead178f0e81a590.text:
## § 169 Messstellen und Messgeräte
The facility designated as the measurement station (§ 169 Abs. 1 StrlSchG) must provide specific dosimeters upon request:
1. To the Radiation Protection Officer: Personal dosimeters required for determining body dose according to § 65 Abs. 1 Satz 1 and § 66 Abs. 1 Nummer 1 [StrlSchV.md].
2. To the party obligated according to § 145 Abs. 2 StrlSchG: Personal dosimeters required for determining body dose according to § 165 Abs. 1 Nummer 1 in conjunction with § 65 Abs. 1 Satz 1 and § 66 Abs. 1 Nummer 1 [StrlSchV.md].
3. To the responsible party according to § 153 Abs. 1 StrlSchG: Personal dosimeters required for determining body dose according to § 166 Abs. 1 Nummer 1 in conjunction with § 65 Abs. 1 Satz 1 and § 66 Abs. 1 Nummer 1 [StrlSchV.md].
4. To the party obligated according to § 131 Abs. 1 StrlSchG: Measuring devices required for determining exposure according to § 157 Abs. 2 Nummer 1 [StrlSchV.md].

The measurement station (§ 169 Abs. 1 StrlSchG) can utilize an accredited body (§ 155 Abs. 3) for the evaluation of measuring devices (§ 157 Abs. 2 Nummer 1), provided the measurement station continues to meet the requirements of § 169 Abs. 2 StrlSchG [StrlSchV.md]. Furthermore, designated measurement stations participate in quality assurance measures conducted by the Federal Institute for Physics and Technology (BPT) for body dose determination (§ 66 Abs. 1 und 2 Satz 3) and by the Federal Office for Radiation Protection (BfS) for body dose determination (§ 65 Abs. 4 und § 157 Abs. 3) [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:2b6df4d7cfc23e0.text:
## § 173 Strahlenschutzregister
The Federal Office for Radiation Protection (BfS) determines the technical procedure for generating and the structure of the personal identification number according to § 170 Abs. 3 StrlSchG [StrlSchV.md]. The BfS can accept an identification number issued by a competent authority outside the scope of the StrlSchG if it meets three criteria:
1. It can be clearly assigned to the monitored person [StrlSchV.md].
2. It remains unchanged throughout the monitored person's lifetime [StrlSchV.md].
3. It is available at the monitored person or the employing company [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:865d947bad22718.text:
## § 174 Strahlenpass (Radiation Pass)
The party responsible for ensuring that specified persons are only occupied with radiation sources (§ 68 Abs. 1, § 158 Abs. 1 Satz 1, § 165 Abs. 2 Nummer 2 oder § 166 Abs. 2 Nummer 2) is responsible for maintaining the Radiation Pass. This party must ensure that the pass is registered with the authority responsible according to § 174 Abs. 2 StrlSchG [StrlSchV.md].

The competent authority registers a Radiation Pass for a period of six years if:
1. The data required by Abs. 3 Nummer 1 Buchstabe a to c and Nummer 4 are entered [StrlSchV.md].
2. The pass is manually signed by the pass holder and the responsible party for maintaining the pass [StrlSchV.md].
3. Sufficient space is provided in the pass for further entries required by Abs. 3 [StrlSchV.md].

The authority can extend the validity of a registered Radiation Pass for up to five years if the requirements of Abs. 2 Satz 1 are met [StrlSchV.md].

The pass must contain, at minimum:
1. From the responsible party: The personal data of the pass holder and personal identification number according to § 170 Abs. 3 StrlSchG; data about the responsible party, including operating number and contact details; the balance sheet of official dose values from professional exposure for every calendar year and every month of the calendar year; and any exceeding of dose limits [StrlSchV.md].
2. From the responsible party for the external facility: Data on the exposure in the external facility, setup, or operating site [StrlSchV.md].
3. From the responsible party or authorized physician: Data on the medical monitoring performed, especially the content of the certificate according to § 79 Abs. 1 [StrlSchV.md].
4. From the authority: Data on the issuance of the pass and the authority itself [StrlSchV.md].

The pass is the property of the pass holder and is non-transferable. Upon termination of employment, the responsible party must ensure the pass is returned to the pass holder. If it cannot be returned, it must be handed over to the registering authority, which can destroy it after ten years following the expiration of its validity [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:46b9f0f974dfc8e.text:
## § 175 Ermächtigte Ärzte (Authorized Physicians)
The competent authority authorizes physicians to perform medical monitoring according to §§ 77, 78, 79, and 81, including in conjunction with §§ 151, 158 Abs. 3, §§ 165, or 166 [StrlSchV.md]. The authorization can only be granted to a physician who proves the specialized knowledge required for medical monitoring in radiation protection for professional exposure. The authorization is limited to five years [StrlSchV.md].

The authorized physician is responsible for conducting initial examinations, repeat examinations, and assessments according to §§ 77 and 78, as well as special medical monitoring according to § 81. They must propose measures necessary for the prevention and defense against health damage in case of increased exposure. Furthermore, they must maintain a health record (§ 79 Abs. 2 StrlSchG) for every person subject to medical monitoring [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:839c7417698c694.text:
## § 176 Duldungspflichten (Duty to Tolerate)
Persons subject to medical monitoring (§§ 77 und 78, etc.) must tolerate the required medical examinations [StrlSchV.md]. Similarly, persons for whom body dose determination (§ 64 Abs. 1 Satz 1 oder Absatz 2, etc.) or contamination determination (§ 57 Abs. 1 oder § 58 Abs. 1) is required must tolerate the necessary measurements and determinations [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:60f17f89865ad45.text:
## § 177 Bestimmung von Sachverständigen (Appointment of Experts)
The competent authority appoints individual experts (§ 172 Abs. 1 StrlSchG) if:
1. There are no facts suggesting doubts regarding the reliability or independence of the applicant [StrlSchV.md].
2. The applicant meets the requirements for training, professional knowledge, and skills required by § 181 [StrlSchV.md].
3. The technical and organizational equipment required for the proper execution of the examination task is available [StrlSchV.md].

The authority appoints expert organizations (§ 172 Abs. 1 StrlSchG) if:
1. There are no facts suggesting doubts regarding the independence of the expert organization [StrlSchV.md].
2. There are no facts suggesting doubts regarding the reliability or independence of the authorized representatives [StrlSchV.md].
3. There are no facts suggesting doubts regarding the reliability of the examining person [StrlSchV.md].
4. The examining person meets the requirements for training, professional knowledge, and skills required by § 181 [StrlSchV.md].
5. The technical and organizational equipment required for the proper execution of the examination task is available [StrlSchV.md].

The appointment as an expert is limited to five years [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:f8c5cbc5b2a62e3.text:
## § 180 Unabhängigkeit (Independence)
Independence is given if the individual expert or the expert organization is not subject to any economic, financial, or other influence that could affect their judgment or call into question trust in the impartial performance of their duties [StrlSchV.md]. Independence is generally not given if the expert or the organization is involved in the development, manufacture, distribution, or maintenance of devices or facilities that are to be examined [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:9cdb722834baf4c.text:
## § 181 Fachliche Qualifikation (Professional Qualification)
An individual expert or examining person must:
1. Possess a university or university of applied sciences degree in a natural science or technical field [StrlSchV.md].
2. Possess the required specialized knowledge in radiation protection [StrlSchV.md].
3. Have been trained in the expert activity by a person who has been active as an individual expert or examining person for at least three years [StrlSchV.md].
4. Have completed examinations according to Annex 19 during the training period [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:9abd662f2f0d89e.text:
## § 182 Prüfmaßstab (Examination Criteria)
The expert must assess the extent to which the technical design, function, and safety of the examined device, facility, or contained radioactive substance, as well as the structural conditions, guarantee the protection of personnel, the population, and the examined or treated persons [StrlSchV.md].

heading_path: § 183 Pflichten des behördlich bestimmten Sachverständigen (Duties of the State-Appointed Expert)
wiki:summary-strlschv.md:c23973423e68a88.text:
## § 183 Pflichten des behördlich bestimmten Sachverständigen (Duties of the State-Appointed Expert)
The state-appointed individual expert is obliged to:
1. Immediately notify the competent authority of any changes affecting the prerequisites for the appointment [StrlSchV.md].
2. Ensure that the measuring devices and testing equipment used are properly maintained, suitable for the respective measurement task, and available in sufficient quantity [StrlSchV.md].
3. Regularly check and maintain the measuring equipment regarding its proper condition and functionality [StrlSchV.md].
4. Participate in the measures of exchange of opinions and experience specified in the appointment decree [StrlSchV.md].
5. Regularly carry out and document the quality assurance measures specified in the appointment decree [StrlSchV.md].
6. Submit a copy of the examination report to the authority responsible for the party obligated to the expert examination within four weeks after an examination [StrlSchV.md].
7. Report to the authority in whose jurisdiction they operate on the subject and scope of their expert activity, either regularly or on special occasion; specifically, records made during every examination must be summarized and presented to the authority upon request annually [StrlSchV.md].
8. Inform the authority responsible for the party obligated to the expert examination, as well as the Radiation Protection Officer or Radiation Protection Officer, immediately if they determine or suspect that life or health of persons or the environment is endangered by the examined device, facility, contained radioactive substance, or the examined workplace with exposure to naturally occurring radioactivity [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:f998444d64e93e3.text:
## § 184 Ordnungswidrigkeiten (Administrative Offenses)
The source lists numerous specific administrative offenses (Ordnungswidrigkeiten) related to non-compliance with the StrlSchG, covering areas such as failure to perform quality control, failure to provide documentation, and improper handling of radioactive substances [StrlSchV.md].
## Legal Status and Scope
*   **Title:** Verordnung zum Schutz vor der schädlichen Wirkung ionisierender Strahlung (Strahlenschutzverordnung - StrlSchV) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Issuance Date:** 29.11.2018 [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Legal Basis:** The StrlSchV implements the EU Directive 2013/59/Euratom and supersedes several older directives, including 89/618/Euratom, 90/641/Euratom, 96/29/Euratom, 97/43/Euratom, and 2003/122/Euratom [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Entry into Force:** The regulation entered into force on December 31, 2018 [StrlSchV.md].
## Key Definitions (Teil 1)
The regulation establishes precise definitions for technical and legal concepts:
*   **Ableitung (Emission):** Defined as the release of liquid, particulate, or gaseous radioactive substances via designated routes [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Äquivalentdosis (Equivalent Dose):** Calculated as the product of the energy dose in ICRU soft tissue and the quality factor Q of the ICRU (according to Anlage 18 Teil D). If multiple radiation types/energies are present, the total equivalent dose is the sum of individual contributions [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Dosisrichtwert (Dose Limit):** Defined as an effective dose or organ-equivalent dose that serves as the upper value for considering exposure during the planning and optimization of protective measures for persons in planned exposure situations [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Vorkommnis (Incident):** An event in a planned exposure situation that has led to, could lead to, or might lead to an unintended exposure. No incident exists if the event is irrelevant for radiation protection [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Störfall (Accident):** A sequence of events where the operation of the nuclear facility, the facility generating ionizing radiation, or the activity cannot be continued for safety reasons, and for which the facility must be shut down or for which precautionary measures must be taken [StrlSchV.md].
## Regulatory Procedures
The StrlSchV details procedures for various exposure scenarios:
### Planned Exposure Situations (Teil 2)
*   **Justification of Activities (§ 2, § 3, § 4):** Activities must be justified. The procedure for checking justification based on § 7 or § 38 of the Radiation Protection Act requires submitting specific documentation (Anlage 2) and involves assessment by the Federal Office for Radiation Protection (BfS) regarding the suitability and fitness of the item, facility, or process [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Exemptions (§ 5 - § 11):** The regulation outlines specific cases where a permit or notification is not required, such as for certain handling of radioactive substances (§ 5) or the operation of certain facilities (§ 7, § 8, § 9) [StrlSchV.md].
### Emergency Exposure Situations (Teil 3)
*   The regulation mandates specific provisions for dosimetry and medical monitoring of emergency response personnel (*Einsatzkräfte*) during accidents or incidents (§ 150, § 151) [StrlSchV.md].
### Existing Exposure Situations (Teil 4)
*   **Radioactive Contaminated Sites (Altlasten):** Procedures are detailed for determining exposure, setting test values, and establishing principles for remediation plans (*Sanierungspläne*) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Waste Management:** Covers the handling of lost, found, or acquired contaminated metal (§ 167-170) and general principles for waste management [StrlSchV.md].
### Cross-Cutting Provisions (Teil 5)
*   **Registers and Passes:** The law mandates the maintenance of the Radiation Protection Register (*Strahlenschutzregister*) (§ 173) and the use of the Radiation Pass (*Strahlenpass*) (§ 174) [StrlSchV.md].
## Transitional Provisions for Permits and Authorizations
The ordinance details how previous authorizations continue to apply under the new legal framework:
*   **Building Permit Approvals (§ 185):** Building permit approved devices containing radioactive substances must be checked for integrity and tightness every ten years after the expiration of the original permit, unless shorter periods were specified. If the permit expired more than ten years before December 31, 2018, the check must occur by December 31, 2021.
*   **Residues (§ 186):** A release granted under the previous version of the StrlSchV (valid until December 31, 2018) remains valid under § 29 if the competent authority grants its consent by June 30, 2019.
*   **Release (§ 187):** Releases granted under the previous StrlSchV (valid until December 31, 2018) remain valid under the new sections, with specific rules applying to the values used (e.g., requiring adherence to values from Anlage 4 Tabelle 1 Spalte 3 starting January 1, 2021).
## Professional Qualifications and Organization
The document outlines requirements for personnel and facilities:
*   **Operational Organization (§ 188):** For facilities operating before December 31, 2018, a contract must be concluded for the operational organization of radiation protection by December 31, 2019. Furthermore, radiation protection instructions must be created or updated by January 1, 2020.
*   **Required Expertise (§ 189):** The ordinance confirms that expertise acquired before specific dates (e.g., August 1, 2001, or July 1, 2002) remains valid. For instance, qualifications established before December 31, 2018, are recognized as continuing until December 31, 2026, for certain courses.
## Dose and Emission Limits (Anlage 4)
Appendix 4 details various exemption and limit values:
*   **Surface Contamination:** For surface contamination measurements (§ 57 and § 58), the sum of the ratio of existing activity ($A_{s,i}$) to the respective surface contamination values ($O_i$) must not exceed 1, provided the proportion of unconsidered nuclides does not exceed 10 percent [StrlSchV.md].
*   **General Limits:** The table provides specific values for various radionuclides (e.g., H-3: 1 E+9 Bq/g; C-14: 1 E+7 Bq/g; Ar-37: 1 E+8 Bq/g).
## Exemptions and Non-Justified Activities (Anlage 1 & 3)
*   **Non-Justified Activities (Anlage 1):** Lists activities that are not justified, including the use of radioactive sources for leak detection (unless collected) and the use of X-rays for checking clothing/shoes [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Exempt Activities (Anlage 3):** Details activities that are exempt from permitting or notification, such as handling substances where the specific activity does not exceed 500 Mikrobecquerel per gram (Part A) [StrlSchV.md].
## Isotope Data and Decay Parameters
The source material presents comprehensive data across numerous isotopes, including:
*   **Half-lives:** Values are provided in various units (days 'd', hours 'h', years 'a', seconds 's', minutes 'm'). Examples include ${}^{135}\text{Cs}$ with a half-life of $2.3 \times 10^6$ years [StrlSchV.md], and ${}^{192}\text{Mo}$ with a half-life of $11.8$ days [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Activity/Concentration:** Several columns detail measured values, often using scientific notation (e.g., $1 \text{ E}+9$, $1 \text{ E}-1$).
*   **Specific Isotopes:** Data is provided for isotopes such as ${}^{90}\text{Sr}$ (Strontium-90), ${}^{137}\text{Cs}$ (Cesium-137), ${}^{60}\text{Co}$ (Cobalt-60), and various isotopes of Iodine ($\text{I}$) and Cesium ($\text{Cs}$) [StrlSchV.md].
## Structure and Scope
The tables are highly structured, listing isotopes alphabetically and providing multiple quantitative measurements across various parameters. The data appears to be used for technical assessments required under the broader radiation protection framework [StrlSchV.md].
***
# Strahlenschutzverordnung - StrlSchV (Teil 12/15)
This document provides detailed excerpts and appendices from the German Radiation Protection Ordinance (*Strahlenschutzverordnung - StrlSchV*), which supplements the *Strahlenschutzgesetz* (StrlSchG). It covers complex technical aspects of radiation protection, including radioactive decay chains, criteria for the clearance of contaminated materials, and principles for calculating public exposure.
## Radioactive Decay Chains (Anlage 4)
The source includes extensive tables detailing the decay chains of various radionuclides, showing parent nuclides and their daughter products (e.g., Pb-210 $\rightarrow$ Bi-210 $\rightarrow$ Po-210) [StrlSchV.md].
**Key Examples of Decay Chains:**
*   **U-238 Series:** The decay chain involving U-238+ leads through several isotopes, including Pb-210+ $\rightarrow$ Bi-210 $\rightarrow$ Po-210 [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Actinium Series:** The decay from Ra-228+ proceeds through Ac-228, Ac-224+, and subsequent daughters, including Th-228+ [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Radon Series:** The decay chain starting from Rn-222+ is detailed, showing multiple subsequent products like Tl-210, Pb-209, and Po-214 [StrlSchV.md].
## Monitoring and Clearance Criteria (Anlage 5)
Appendix 5 sets the monitoring limits and disposal pathways for residues.
1.  **General Monitoring Limit:** For the evaluation of residues, the sum of the maximum specific activities of U-238 and Th-232 must satisfy the formula: $C_{U238max} + C_{Th232max} \leq C$ [StrlSchV.md].
    *   The monitoring limit $C$ is set to $1 \text{ Bq/g}$ for dry mass.
    *   If dry mass cannot be determined (e.g., oils), a suitable procedure must be used [StrlSchV.md].
2.  **Alternative Limits:** Lower limits apply if:
    *   More than 5,000 Megagramm of residues are deposited in a usable groundwater catchment area in a calendar year, or if more than 50 percent of residues are added to construction materials in sports/play areas [StrlSchV.md].
    *   The residues are disposed of underground, the limit is $C_{U238max} + C_{Th232max} \leq 5 \text{ Bq/g}$ [StrlSchV.md].
3.  **Factor R Adjustment:** If the largest specific activity of Pb-210 and its relevant daughter products exceeds the largest specific activity of other U-238 series radionuclides by a factor $A > 5$, the limit is adjusted by a factor $R$ (e.g., $R=0.5$ for surface disposal) [StrlSchV.md].
## Exposure Assessment Principles (Anlage 6 & 7)
Appendix 6 outlines the general principles for determining exposure when residues are handled.
*   **Exposure Pathways:** When assessing exposure for individuals or professionals, realistic exposure pathways and assumptions must be used [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Comprehensive Inclusion:** For residues, all potential exposures arising from the planned disposal or recycling route must be included [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Groundwater Deposition (Anlage 7):** For common disposal, the assumption is that exposures will not exceed an effective dose rate of $1 \text{ mSv}$ per calendar year, provided the sum of specific activities meets certain criteria based on the disposal area size (e.g., $C_M = 0.05 \text{ Bq/g}$ for areas $> 15 \text{ Hektar}$) [StrlSchV.md].
## Site-Specific Clearance (Anlage 8)
Appendix 8 details specific clearance values for various materials:
*   **Building/Room Clearance:** Surface contamination values must not exceed the values specified in Anlage 4 Tabelle 1 Spalte 12 if future reuse is not excluded, or Spalte 13 if the structure is to be demolished [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Soil Clearance:** For surface contamination, the averaging area can be up to $100 \text{ m}^2$. Alternatively, for mass-based clearance, the averaging mass can be up to 1 Megagramm [StrlSchV.md].
## Radioactive Scrap Metal Recycling
The document outlines specific conditions for the recycling of radioactive scrap metal:
*   A release of scrap metal for recycling requires that the scrap metal, for which a determination was made according to § 42 Absatz 1, must be smelted [StrlSchV.md].
*   The values listed in Anlage 4 Tabelle 1 Spalte 14 do not apply to composites made of metallic and non-metallic components [StrlSchV.md].
*   Only smelting plants are suitable that can guarantee a mixing ratio of 1:10 of released scrap metal to other metals or that have a throughput of at least 40,000 Megagramm in a calendar year [StrlSchV.md].
*   For a release of scrap metal for recycling contaminated with a single radionuclide from the list (Be-7, C-14, Mn-53, Mn-54, Co-57, Ni-59, Ni-63, Nb-93m, Mo-93, Tc-97, Tc-99, Ru-103, Ag-105, Ag-108m, Cd-109, Sb-125, Te-132, I-129, Eu-155, Ti-204, Pa-231, Es-254 or Fm-255), the mass is limited to 10 Megagramm in a calendar year. A contamination with a single radionuclide exists if all other radionuclides together do not exceed an activity fraction of one thousandth [StrlSchV.md].
## Dose Calculation Parameters (Anlage 11)
The document details the assumptions for calculating exposure (Expositionspfade) [StrlSchV.md]:
**Part A: Exposure Pathways**
1.  **With Air:**
    *   1.1 Exposure through beta radiation within the exhaust plume (Betasubmersion) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   1.2 Exposure through gamma radiation from the exhaust plume (Gammasubmersion) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   1.3 Exposure through gamma radiation of radioactive substances deposited on the ground (Gammabodenstrahlung) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   1.4 Exposure through inhalation of radioactive substances (Inhalation) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   1.5 Exposure through ingestion of radioactive substances (Ingestion) via food [StrlSchV.md].
        *   1.5.1 Air – Plant (Luft – Pflanze) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   1.5.2 Air – Feed Plant – Cow – Milk (Luft – Futterpflanze – Kuh – Milch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   1.5.3 Air – Feed Plant – Animal – Meat (Luft – Futterpflanze – Tier – Fleisch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   1.5.4 Air – Breast Milk (Luft – Muttermilch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   1.5.5 Air – Food – Breast Milk (Luft – Nahrung – Muttermilch) [StrlSchV.md].
2.  **With Water:**
    *   2.1 Exposure through staying on sediment (Gammabodenstrahlung) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   2.2 Exposure through ingestion of radioactive substances (Ingestion) via food [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.1 Drinking Water (Trinkwasser) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.2 Water – Fish (Wasser – Fisch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.3 Livestock Water – Cow – Milk (Viehtränke – Kuh – Milch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.4 Livestock Water – Animal – Meat (Viehtränke – Tier – Fleisch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.5 Rainfall – Feed Plant – Cow – Milk (Beregnung – Futterpflanze – Kuh – Milch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.6 Rainfall – Feed Plant – Animal – Meat (Beregnung – Futterpflanze – Tier – Fleisch) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.7 Rainfall – Plant (Beregnung – Pflanze) [StrlSchV.md].
        *   2.2.8 Breast Milk due to ingestion of radioactive substances by the mother via the above mentioned ingestion pathways (Muttermilch infolge der Aufnahme radioaktiver Stoffe durch die Mutter über die oben genannten Ingestionspfade) [StrlSchV.md].
3.  **From Nuclear Facilities/Radiation Sources:**
    *   3.1 Gamma radiation (Gammastrahlung) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   3.2 X-ray radiation (Röntgenstrahlung) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   3.3 Neutron radiation (Neutronenstrahlung) [StrlSchV.md].
4.  **From Contaminated Ground:**
    *   4.1 Exposure through staying on contaminated grounds or next to contaminated deposits (Gammabodenstrahlung) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   4.2 Exposure through inhalation of resuspended soil or material from deposits (Staubinhalation) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   4.3 Exposure through ingestion of contaminated soil or material from deposits via the mouth (Bodeningestion) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   4.4 Exposure through ingestion of food produced in private gardens next to contaminated deposits (dust path) or on contaminated grounds (Staubpfad) [StrlSchV.md].
*Note: Exposure pathways remain disregarded or additional exposure pathways (e.g., the seepage water path in contaminated deposits) must be considered if this is due to local site specifics or the type of nuclear facility/radiation source [StrlSchV.md].*
**Part B: Consumption Rates (Verzehrsraten)**
The document provides tables for average consumption rates of representative persons in kg/a across various food groups and age groups (e.g., Drinking Water: 551 kg/a for $\le 1$ year; Milk, Milk Products: 160 kg/a for $> 1 – \le 2$ years) [StrlSchV.md].
**Part C: Breathing Rates (Atemraten)**
Breathing rates are provided in $\text{m}^3/\text{Year}$ for different age groups (e.g., $\le 1$ year: 1 $\text{m}^3/\text{Year}$; $> 17$ years: 8,100 $\text{m}^3/\text{Year}$) [StrlSchV.md].
**Part D: Exposure Calculation Factors**
*   **Table 3 (Aufenthaltsdauern, Aufenthaltsorte und Reduktionsfaktoren):** Provides exposure durations and locations (e.g., Beta radiation within the exhaust plume: 1,760 hours/year outdoors – 7,000 hours/year indoors) and reduction factors (e.g., Gamma radiation from the exhaust plume: 0.3) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Table 4 (Generische radionuklidspezifische Faktoren):** Provides generic radionuclide-specific factors (e.g., Gammabodenstrahlung: 2 for I-125, I-131) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Table 5 (Expositionspfadspezifische Faktoren):** Provides pathway-specific factors (e.g., Ingestion of food: 3) [StrlSchV.md].
## Radionuclide Activity Concentrations ($C_i$)
The document contains extensive tables detailing maximal permissible activity concentrations ($C_i$) for various radionuclides in air and water, categorized by binding state (A = adsorbed to particulate matter, E = elemental, O = organic) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Table 6:** Lists $C_i$ for radionuclides like H-3, Be-7, C-14, etc., in air and water [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Subsequent Tables:** Provide comprehensive data for dozens of radionuclides (e.g., Cs-137, I-131, Sr-90, etc.) detailing concentrations in $\text{Bq}/\text{m}^3$ for both air and water [StrlSchV.md].
## Key Appendices and Criteria
### Anlage 14 (zu § 108) – Criteria for Significance in Medical/Non-Medical Exposure
This appendix sets criteria for determining the significance of an event during medical exposure or non-medical application [StrlSchV.md].
**I. Investigations without Interventions (Excluding conventional radiography and digital dental/cranial tomography):**
*   **Group Level:** An exceedance of the average over the last 20 consecutive investigations of the same type at the same device by more than 100 percent of the respective diagnostic reference value, provided the diagnostic reference value of a single investigation was exceeded by 200 percent.
*   **Individual Level:**
    *   Exceedance of the volume-based CT dose index in the brain of 120 Milligray, or for other CT applications of 80 Milligray, or the total dose-area product of a plain X-ray of 20,000 Centigray-cm² [StrlSchV.md]. For digital volume tomography, the first exceeded value (CT or plain X-ray) applies.
    *   Exceedance of the intended effective dose by more than 20 Millisieverts or an organ dose by more than 100 Millisieverts from radioactive substances during a single investigation.
    *   Other criteria include repeat applications (due to misidentification, setting error, or device defect) or misidentification of persons/body parts, if the resulting additional total exposure meets the criteria above [StrlSchV.md].
    *   Occurrence of a deterministic effect not expected for the specified investigation.
**II. Interventions:**
*   **Group Level:** Exceedance of the average over the last 20 consecutive interventions of the same type by more than 100 percent of the respective diagnostic reference value, provided the diagnostic reference value of a single investigation was exceeded by 200 percent.
*   **Individual Level (For investigation purposes):**
    *   Exceedance of the total dose-area product of 20,000 Centigray-cm² [StrlSchV.md].
    *   Other criteria include repeat applications or misidentification of persons/body parts, if the resulting additional total exposure meets the criteria above [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Individual Level (For treatment purposes):**
    *   Exceedance of the total dose-area product of 50,000 Centigray-cm² if a deterministic skin damage of the second or higher grade occurs acutely or within 21 days after the interventional investigation.
    *   Other criteria include misidentification of persons/body parts or occurrence of an unexpected deterministic effect [StrlSchV.md].
**III. Treatments with Ionizing Radiation/Enclosed Radioactive Substances:**
*   Deviation of the total dose in the target volume or at the reference point by more than 10 percent from the dose specified in the irradiation plan, provided the deviation is at least 4 Gray.
*   Unplanned exceedance of the dose limitation specified in the working instructions for risk organs, if the exceedance is more than 10 percent.
*   Deviation of the mean total dose by more than 10 percent from the specified mean dose in the target volume or for risk organs.
*   Deviation from the total treatment time specified in the irradiation plan by more than one week, unless caused by the treated person.
*   Misidentification of persons or irradiation plans.
*   Occurrence of a deterministic effect not expected for the specified treatment.
**IV. Treatments with Open Radioactive Substances:**
*   Deviation of the administered total activity by more than 10 percent from the specified activity.
*   Occurrence of a deterministic effect not expected for the specified treatment.
*   Misidentification of persons or body parts, or misidentification of the radioactive substance.
*   Occurrence of a paravasate after injection of the radioactive substance, if more than 15 percent of the intended activity was misapplied.
*   Contamination by a radioactive substance, if an unintentional exposure of the treated person occurred and the resulting effective dose exceeded 20 Millisieverts or the organ equivalent dose exceeded 100 Millisieverts.
**V. Care and Accompanying Persons:**
*   Any unintentional exceedance of the effective dose of 1 Millisievert for a care and accompanying person.
**VI. Medical Research:**
*   For applications approved under § 31 StrlSchG: Any significant occurrence according to the criteria in Sections I to V.
*   For applications shown under § 32 StrlSchG: Any significant occurrence according to the criteria in Sections I, II, and V.
*   For investigations for medical research: Any exceedance of the dose limits according to § 137 Paragraph 2 or 3 StrlSchG [StrlSchV.md].
**VII. Near Misses:**
*   Any discovered event of near-miss exposure outside of quality assurance measures for which one of the criteria of Sections I to VI would apply if the exposure had actually occurred.
### Anlage 15 (zu § 108) – Criteria for Significance in Planned Exposure Situations
This appendix defines criteria for planned exposure situations [StrlSchV.md]:
1. Exposure of a professionally exposed person who exceeds a body dose limit—effective dose or organ equivalent dose—according to § 78 StrlSchG, unless the exposure is a particularly permitted exposure according to § 74 StrlSchG.
2. Exposure of an individual in the population who exceeds a limit according to § 80 StrlSchG.
3. Exceedance of the permissible derivation of radioactive substances with air or water.
4. Release of radioactive substances:
    a) within an area marked as a control area, unless it is marked as a exclusion area, if the site dose rate exceeds 3 Millisieverts per hour for more than 24 hours.
    b) within a monitoring area, requiring the establishment of a new control area, or
    c) into the environment with activities above the release limits according to Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 2.
5. Contamination:
    a) Contamination within a control area, in an area that cannot be contaminated by design, exceeding one thousand times the values of Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 5, and whose total activity in Becquerel is more than one hundred times the values of Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 2.
    b) Contamination within a monitoring area, in an area that cannot be contaminated by design, exceeding one hundred times the values of Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 5 and whose total activity in Becquerel is more than ten times the values of Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 2, or
    c) Contamination not covered by letters a or b, exceeding ten times the values of Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 5 and whose total activity in Becquerel exceeds the values of Anlage 4 Table 1 Column 2.
6. Extraordinary event sequence or operating condition of significant safety engineering importance during the operation of an X-ray facility, a licensed scatter source, facilities for generating ionizing radiation, or during the handling or transport of radioactive substances.
### Anlage 16 (zu § 149) – Risk Assessment Criteria
This appendix outlines criteria for determining the type and extent of risk associated with an activity [StrlSchV.md]:
Regular on-site inspections by a radiation protection officer must be based on a systematic assessment of the risks associated with the activity, particularly using the following criteria:
1. Expected exposure during normal use of ionizing radiation or radioactive substances to humans.
2. Expected exposure during normal use of ionizing radiation or radioactive substances in applications without targeted exposure to persons.
3. Activity level of the licensed handling of enclosed and open radioactive substances.
4. Risk of incorporations when handling open radioactive substances.
5. Risk of unintentional exposures.
6. Available protective facilities to prevent unintentional exposures in X-ray facilities, scatter sources, facilities for generating ionizing radiation, and enclosed radioactive substances, as well as the scope of required radiation protection measures for the safe execution of activities.
7. Other risk-relevant conditions for activities according to § 4 Paragraph 1 StrlSchG in planned exposure situations.
### Anlage 17 (zu § 159) – Activity Index
This appendix defines the Activity Index ($I$) for building materials, considering the building material density ($\rho$) in $\text{kg/m}^3$ and the building material thickness ($d$) in meters, using the specific activities of Radium-226 ($^{226}\text{Ra}$), Thorium-232 (or its decay product Radium-228) ($^{232}\text{Th}$), and Potassium-40 ($^{40}\text{K}$) in the building material in $\text{Bq/kg}$ [StrlSchV.md].
*   If the surface density $\rho \cdot d$ exceeds 500 $\text{kg/m}^2$, the value 500 $\text{kg/m}^2$ is used instead.
*   The reference value of 1 millisievert per year is considered maintained if the Activity Index $I$ does not exceed 1.
*   For thin-layer materials (thickness up to 0.03 meters) used only in combination with a supporting or supporting surface (wall, ceiling, floor)—e.g., tiles—a contribution of 0.48 is to be added to the index for general consideration of the underlying surface.
*   If the building material thickness in the structure is unknown, $d = 0.2$ meters is to be set.
### Anlage 18 (zu den §§ 171, 197) – Dose and Measurement Units
This appendix details measurement quantities for external radiation (Teil A) and body dose calculation (Teil B) [StrlSchV.md].
**Part A: Measurement Quantities for External Radiation:**
1. **For Person Dose:** Depth-person dose $H_p(10)$, Eye lens-person dose $H_p(3)$, and Surface-person dose $H_p(0.07)$ [StrlSchV.md].
2. **For Site Dose:** Ambient equivalent dose $H^*(10)$, directional equivalent dose in 3 mm depth $H'(3,\Omega)$, and directional equivalent dose in 0.07 mm depth $H'(0,07,\Omega)$ [StrlSchV.md].
**Part B: Calculation of Body Dose:**
1. **Organ Equivalent Dose $H_T$:** $H_{T,R} = w_R D_{T,R}$, where $D_{T,R}$ is the mean energy dose generated by radiation $R$ in the tissue or organ $T$, and $w_R$ is the radiation weighting factor from Part C Number 1 [StrlSchV.md].
2. **Effective Dose $E$:** The effective dose is the weighted mean of organ equivalent doses, considering the radiation sensitivity of various organs or tissues via weighting factors $w_T$ from Part C Number 2 [StrlSchV.md].
## Radionuclide Activity Concentration Tables
The source contains extensive tables detailing the activity concentrations ($\text{Ci}$) of various radionuclides in air ($\text{Bq/m}^3$) and water ($\text{Bq/m}^3$) across multiple pages (Pages 161 through 172). These tables list specific isotopes (e.g., $\text{Sm-153}$, $\text{Dy-155}$, $\text{Pb-214}$, $\text{U-238}$, $\text{Pu-239}$, $\text{Th-232}$, etc.) and their measured concentrations under different binding states (A = adsorbed, E = elemental, O = organic) [StrlSchV.md].
## Dosimetry Calculations
The source details the calculation of effective dose, particularly considering the follow-up dose ($E(\tau)$) resulting from radionuclides remaining in the body after the reference point [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Effective Follow-up Dose ($E(\tau)$):** This is the sum of the follow-up organ equivalent doses ($H_T(\tau)$) multiplied by the corresponding tissue weighting factor ($w_T$) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Time Period ($\tau$):** For adults, the integration period is set at 50 years; for children, it is from the respective age until age 70, unless another value is specified [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Dose Calculation Methods:**
    *   **Inhalation of Radon at Workplaces:** An effective dose of 1 millisievert is assumed from:
        a) A Radon-222 exposure of 0.32 Megabecquerel per cubic meter per hour, using a balance factor of 0.4 between Radon-222 and its short-lived decay products, OR
        b) A potential Alpha energy exposure of 0.71 millijoule per cubic meter per hour [StrlSchV.md].
    *   **Incorporation, Submersion, or Soil Contamination:** Dose coefficients and guidelines must be drawn from specific publications in the *Bundesanzeiger* (e.g., No. 160 a and b from August 28, 2001, Part I and Part II, and the *Bundesanzeiger* of May 10, 2023) [StrlSchV.md].
    *   **Unborn Child:** Specific dose coefficients are required for external exposure (using the *Bundesanzeiger* announcement of April 17, 2023, BAnz AT 10. Mai 2023 B6) and for internal exposure due to radionuclide incorporation [StrlSchV.md].
## Weighting Factors
The source defines three critical weighting factors used in dosimetry:
### 1. Radiation Weighting Factor ($w_R$)
These values depend on the type and quality of the external radiation field or the radiation emitted by an incorporated radionuclide [StrlSchV.md]:
| Radiation Type | Radiation Weighting Factor ($w_R$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Photons | 1 |
| Electrons and Muons | 1 |
| Protons and Charged Pions | 2 |
| Alpha particles, Fragment particles, Heavy Ions | 20 |
| Neutrons, Energy $E_n < 1$ | |
| Neutrons, $1 \le \text{Energy } E_n \le 50$ | |
| Neutrons, Energy $E_n > 50$ | |
### 2. Tissue Weighting Factor ($w_T$)
These factors are used for calculating the effective dose [StrlSchV.md]:
| Tissue or Organ | Tissue Weighting Factor ($w_T$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. Red Bone Marrow | 0.12 |
| 2. Large Intestine | 0.12 |
| 3. Lungs | 0.12 |
| 4. Stomach | 0.12 |
| 5. Breast | 0.12 |
| 6. Gonads | 0.08 |
| 7. Bladder | 0.04 |
| 8. Esophagus | 0.04 |
| 9. Liver | 0.04 |
| 10. Thyroid | 0.04 |
| 11. Skin | 0.01 |
| 12. Bone Surface | 0.01 |
| 13. Brain | 0.01 |
| 14. Salivary Glands | 0.01 |
| 15. Other Organs or Tissues | 0.12 |
### 3. Quality Factor ($Q$)
The values for the Quality Factor $Q$ (according to ICRP 2007) depend on the unconstrained linear energy transfer potential $L$ in water [StrlSchV.md]:
| $L$ | $Q(L)$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $< 10$ | 1 |
| $10 \le L \le 100$ | $0.32 \cdot L^{-2.2}$ |
| $L > 100$ | $300/\sqrt{L}$ |
## Qualification for Experts (Sachverständige)
The source provides detailed tables outlining the required number of examinations for acquiring and maintaining the professional qualification for radiation safety experts ($\text{Sachverständige}$) according to $\S 172$ of the Radiation Protection Act (StrlSchG) [StrlSchV.md].
**Table 1: Examinations for Qualification under $\S 172$ Paragraph 1 Sentence 1 Number 1, 3, and 4 StrlSchG**
*   **A Medical and Dental X-ray Equipment:**
    *   *Acquisition:* Requires examination of 1 fixed and 1 mobile X-ray device.
    *   *Maintenance:* Requires examination of 1.1 and 1.2 devices.
*   **A 2 Fluoroscopy Equipment:**
    *   *Acquisition:* 30 systems; *Maintenance:* 15 systems.
*   **A 3 CT Equipment:**
    *   *Acquisition:* 10 systems; *Maintenance:* 5 systems.
*   **B Non-Medical X-ray Equipment and Scatter Sources:**
    *   *B 1 Fine and Coarse Structure Examination Equipment:* *Acquisition:* 20 systems; *Maintenance:* 10 systems.
    *   *B 2 High-, Full-Protection, and Basic Protection Equipment and School X-ray Equipment:* *Acquisition:* 5 systems; *Maintenance:* 2 systems.
**Table 2: Examinations for Qualification under $\S 172$ Paragraph 1 Sentence 1 Number 2 StrlSchG**
*   This table details requirements for various systems (D, E, F), including medical systems (D 1, D 2) and non-medical systems (E 1, E 2, E 2a, E 3, E 4), specifying the required number of examinations for acquisition and maintenance.
## Cross-Border Transport of Radioactive Materials
The ordinance specifies conditions under which radioactive materials can be transported across borders, particularly concerning non-EU member states.
### Requirements for Non-EU Transport
A permit is required for transporting radioactive substances that are not merely temporarily used within the scope of an approved handling process, if the destination state is not an EU member state [StrlSchV.md]. Specific criteria include:
*   **High-Level Sources:** Sources whose activity equals or exceeds ten times the value for high-level radioactive sources in Annex 4 Table 1 Column 4, or those lacking required markings or documentation [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Other Substances/Fuel:** Other radioactive substances (§ 3 Abs. 1 StrlSchG) or nuclear fuels (§ 3 Abs. 3 StrlSchG) whose activity per shipment equals or exceeds 108 times the release limits in Annex 4 Table 1 Column 2 [StrlSchV.md].
### Notification Requirements
For transporting other radioactive substances or nuclear fuels between non-EU states, or from the German jurisdiction to a non-EU state, if no permit is required, the transport must be electronically notified to the competent authority (§ 13 Abs. 1) [StrlSchV.md].
## Technical Requirements for Type Approval (Bauartzulassung)
The StrlSchV sets detailed technical standards for the approval of equipment generating or containing radioactive substances.
### Equipment Containing Other Radioactive Substances (§ 16)
A device containing other radioactive substances (§ 3 Abs. 1 StrlSchG) may only be approved if:
1.  It contains substances that are enclosed and protected from contact [StrlSchV.md].
2.  The ambient dose rate at a distance of 0.1 meter from the touchable surface does not exceed 1 microsievert per hour under normal operating conditions [StrlSchV.md].
3.  The design ensures safe containment, and no further leak tests are required beyond manufacturer quality control and potential leak testing (§ 25 Abs. 4) [StrlSchV.md].
4.  The activity does not exceed ten times the release limits in Annex 4 Table 1 Column 2 [StrlSchV.md].
### Specific Equipment Types
The ordinance details requirements for various devices:
*   **Scatter Sources (§ 17):** Must not exceed 1 microsievert per hour at 0.1 meter from the touchable surface, and operation must rely on safety devices [StrlSchV.md].
*   **X-ray Sources (§ 18):** Requirements vary based on application (human/animal) and voltage, specifying dose rates at 1 meter and 0.1 meter [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Basic Protection Devices (§ 19):** Must enclose the object and maintain a dose rate of 10 microsievert per hour at 0.1 meter from the touchable surface [StrlSchV.md].
*   **High Protection Devices (§ 20):** Must fully enclose the object and maintain a dose rate of 10 microsievert per hour at 0.1 meter from the touchable surface (excluding internal areas) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Full Protection Devices (§ 21):** Must fully enclose the object and maintain a dose rate of 3 microsievert per hour at 0.1 meter from the touchable surface [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Waste Generation Devices (§ 23):** Must ensure that radioactive material generation via activation is excluded, and the dose rate must not exceed 3 microsievert per hour at 0.1 meter [StrlSchV.md].
## Management of Radioactive Residues (Rückstände)
The StrlSchV establishes comprehensive rules for the handling, release, and disposal of residues.
### Release Criteria (Freigabe)
*   **General Release Criterion:** For materials and objects to be used, recycled, or disposed of as non-radioactive, the effective dose for individuals must not exceed 10 microsievert per calendar year [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Specific Releases:** Criteria are set for different waste types, including building rubble (Bauschutt) and soil, referencing specific Annex 4 Table 1 columns (e.g., 1000 Megagramm limit for rubble) [StrlSchV.md].
### Disposal and Recycling
*   **Waste Disposal:** Requires an explanation of the future fate of the waste and an acceptance declaration from the disposal facility operator [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Recycling:** Specific criteria apply for metal scrap recycling, including adherence to Annex 8 Part A Number 1 and Part G [StrlSchV.md].
## Owner Obligations
The owner of a type approval must maintain a quality assurance system, conduct quality control before delivery, and provide specific documentation to the buyer, including the operating manual [StrlSchV.md].
## Operational Duties and Responsibilities
### Duties of the Responsible Person (Inhaber einer Freigabe)
*   **§ 42 Pflichten des Inhabers einer Freigabe:** The Radiation Protection Responsible Person (Strahlenschutzverantwortliche) must confirm the conformity of any material used, processed, stored, or transferred as non-radioactive material with the content of the release notice.
*   **§ 42 (2):** Measurements required to confirm conformity (Freimessungen) and their results must be documented by the responsible person.
*   **§ 42 (3):** The responsible person must immediately inform the competent authority if any requirement upon which the release depends is no longer met.
### Radiation Protection Officer and Instruction
*   **§ 43 Pflichten des Strahlenschutzbeauftragten:** The Radiation Protection Officer must ensure compliance with the duties assigned to the responsible person by this ordinance, provided the corresponding tasks and powers have been transferred according to § 70 Absatz 2 des Strahlenschutzgesetzes.
*   **§ 45 Strahlenschutzanweisung:** The responsible person must ensure that a Radiation Protection Instruction (Strahlenschutzanweisung) is issued. This instruction can be part of other required operating instructions (e.g., occupational safety, emissions protection). It must specify:
    *   Protective measures required in the operation.
    *   A plan for the organization of radiation protection, including designating responsible officers.
    *   Rules for determining dose limits for employees and other persons.
    *   The maintenance of an operating logbook.
    *   Rules for avoiding, investigating, and reporting incidents.
    *   Regular functional testing and maintenance of equipment.
    *   Rules for protection against interference or loss of radioactive materials.
    *   A plan for regular alarm drills and emergency response.
*   **§ 45 (3):** The Radiation Protection Instruction must be updated immediately upon substantial changes.
## Radiation Protection Areas (Strahlenschutzbereiche)
The ordinance details the establishment, demarcation, and monitoring of restricted areas:
*   **§ 52 Einrichten von Strahlenschutzbereichen:** Radiation protection areas must be established for activities where the exposure of persons can exceed the limits for individuals according to § 80 Absatz 1 and 2 of the Radiation Protection Act.
    *   **Überwachungsbereich (Monitoring Area):** Required if persons can receive an effective dose of more than 1 mSv or an organ-equivalent dose of more than 50 mSv for hands, forearms, feet, or ankles, or a local skin dose of more than 50 mSv over a calendar year.
    *   **Kontrollbereich (Control Area):** Required if persons can receive an effective dose of more than 6 mSv or an organ-equivalent dose of more than 15 mSv for the eye lens or 150 mSv for hands, or a local skin dose of more than 150 mSv over a calendar year.
    *   **Sperrbereich (Exclusion Area):** Required if the site dose rate can be higher than 3 mSv per hour.
*   **§ 53 Abgrenzung, Kennzeichnung und Sicherung:** Control areas must be clearly marked with "Kontrollbereich." Exclusion areas must be marked with "Sperrbereich – Kein Zutritt" and secured so that people cannot enter uncontrolled.
*   **§ 55 Zutritt zu Strahlenschutzbereichen:** Access is restricted. For example, entry to a Control Area requires the person to be performing operations or requiring the application of radiation to themselves or a care/accompanying person, and requires consent from a qualified medical professional.
*   **§ 56 Messtechnische Überwachung:** The responsible person must ensure that the site dose or dose rate, the concentration of radioactive substances in the air, or the contamination of the workplace is measured. Records must be kept for at least five years.
*   **§ 57 Kontamination und Dekontamination:** Measures must be taken if contamination is found, especially if the non-adherent surface contamination exceeds specific thresholds (e.g., 100 times the values of Anlage 4 Tabelle 1 Spalte 5 in Control Areas).
## Personnel and Qualifications
*   **§ 47 Erforderliche Fachkunde:** The required expertise must be proven by a certificate issued by the competent authority. Proof requires:
    1.  Evidence of suitable training for the respective application area.
    2.  Proof of practical experience.
    3.  Proof of successful participation in recognized courses.
*   **§ 48 Aktualisierung der Fachkunde:** The required expertise must be updated at least every five years through recognized courses or other suitable continuing education measures.
*   **§ 63 Unterweisung:** The responsible person must ensure that certain persons receive instruction (Unterweisung) covering working methods, possible dangers, safety measures, and data processing related to dose limits. This instruction must be repeated at least once a year.
# Strahlenschutzverordnung - StrlSchV (Teil 4/15)
This document provides excerpts from the *Strahlenschutzverordnung* (StrlSchV), detailing specific regulations concerning occupational exposure monitoring, dose measurement procedures, and the classification of professionally exposed persons.

heading_path: § 64 Pflicht zur Ermittlung der Körperdosis; zu überwachende Personen
wiki:summary-strlschv.md:4d2cc0c421ca80d.text:
## § 64 Pflicht zur Ermittlung der Körperdosis; zu überwachende Personen
The responsible radiation protection officer (*Strahlenschutzverantwortliche*) must ensure that the body dose is determined for persons staying in a radiation protection area, according to § 65 Abs. 1 [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Exemption from Body Dose Measurement:** Body dose determination can be waived for persons in a monitoring area if it is not expected that the effective dose in a calendar year exceeds 1 millisievert (mSv), the organ-equivalent dose for the eye lens exceeds 15 mSv, or the local skin dose exceeds 50 mSv. This exemption does not apply if the competent authority requires it. For stays in a control area, the exemption applies if the competent authority consents [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Non-Area Activities:** Paragraph 1 applies analogously to persons performing activities not connected to a radiation protection area if they can receive an effective dose exceeding 1 mSv, an organ-equivalent dose for the eye lens exceeding 15 mSv, or a local skin dose exceeding 50 mSv in the calendar year [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Flight Personnel:** For flight personnel, Paragraph 1 applies analogously if the effective dose received during the flight from cosmic radiation can exceed 1 mSv in the calendar year. The flight time also includes the positioning time according to § 13 Satz 1 of the Second Implementing Ordinance to the Airworthiness Code [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Documentation:** The responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that the professional exposure received by every person under their supervision is provided in writing upon request, unless a radiation pass is maintained. For aircraft operations requiring notification, the professional exposure must also be provided in writing once per calendar year and after the last deployment for flight personnel [StrlSchV.md].

heading_path: § 65 Vorgehen bei der Ermittlung der Körperdosis
wiki:summary-strlschv.md:1c42fe35a4b6e30.text:
## § 65 Vorgehen bei der Ermittlung der Körperdosis
The responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that the body dose is determined using the person dose according to § 66 [StrlSchV.md]. The competent authority may determine that, in addition to or instead of Paragraph 1, the following are measured:
1.  The site dose, the site dose rate, the concentration of radioactive substances in the air, or the contamination of the workplace [StrlSchV.md].
2.  The body activity or the activity of excretions [StrlSchV.md].
3.  Other properties of the radiation field or the source of ionizing radiation [StrlSchV.md].
If a measurement is omitted or faulty, the competent authority must be informed, and the dose must be estimated. The competent authority sets a substitute dose, which must be transmitted to the Radiation Protection Register according to § 170 of the Radiation Protection Act [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:33bfc8d0adec813.text:
## § 66 Messung der Personendosis
The person dose measurement must be performed using:
1.  A dosimeter available from a site specified according to § 169 of the Radiation Protection Act, or
2.  A dosimeter evaluated under the responsibility of the radiation protection officer, and whose use has been permitted by the competent authority following the consent of a site specified according to § 169 [StrlSchV.md].
The dosimeter must be worn at a site on the body surface considered representative for the exposure, usually on the front of the torso. The dosimeter reading is to be interpreted as a measure of the effective dose if the body dose for individual body parts, organs, or tissues has not been determined more precisely. If it is foreseeable that the organ-equivalent dose for the hands, forearms, feet, or ankles, or the local skin dose, may exceed 150 mSv in a calendar year, or the organ-equivalent dose of the eye lens may exceed 15 mSv, the responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that the person dose is determined by additional dosimeters on individual body parts. The competent authority may order that the person dose be measured by another suitable or by two independent procedures based on the exposure conditions [StrlSchV.md].

heading_path: § 67 Ermittlung der Körperdosis des fliegenden Personals
wiki:summary-strlschv.md:98d6132518cdb48.text:
## § 67 Ermittlung der Körperdosis des fliegenden Personals
Differently from § 65, the responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that for the body dose determination of flight personnel during the operation of an aircraft requiring notification, a calculation program or a suitable measuring device recognized by the competent authority is used [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:d9e98fbb02cb789.text:
## § 68 Beschäftigung mit Strahlenpass
A person who is the radiation protection officer based on a permit according to § 25 Abs. 1, or based on a notification according to § 26 Abs. 1 or § 59 Abs. 2 of the Radiation Protection Act, must ensure that persons under their supervision are only employed in foreign radiation protection areas if every single professionally exposed person possesses a completely maintained and registered radiation pass with the competent authority. This does not apply to radiation protection areas where body dose determination can be waived [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:cff0ec6b36a0eb2.text:
## § 71 Kategorien beruflich exponierter Personen
The responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that professionally exposed persons are assigned to one of the following categories before commencing their activity for control and medical monitoring:
1.  **Category A:** Persons exposed to professional exposure from activities that can lead to an effective dose exceeding 6 mSv, an organ-equivalent dose for the eye lens exceeding 15 mSv, or 150 mSv for the hands, forearms, feet, or ankles, or a local skin dose exceeding 150 mSv in a calendar year [StrlSchV.md].
2.  **Category B:** Persons not classified in Category A and exposed to professional exposure from activities that can lead to an effective dose exceeding 1 mSv, an organ-equivalent dose for the hands, forearms, feet, or ankles exceeding 50 mSv, or a local skin dose exceeding 50 mSv in a calendar year [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:d28d6a7bd7929df.text:
## § 72 Dosisrichtwerte bei Tätigkeiten
The responsible radiation protection officer must check within six months of commencing an activity whether the determination of dose reference values for professionally exposed persons is a suitable instrument for optimizing radiation protection. If dose reference values are set, they must be set for the effective dose or for an organ-equivalent dose for individual persons and related to a period [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:936ec2684f633e2.text:
## § 73 Dosisbegrenzung bei Überschreitung von Grenzwerten
If a limit was exceeded due to a violation of § 78 of the Radiation Protection Act, continued employment as a professionally exposed person is only permissible if the responsible radiation protection officer ensures that the exposures in the following four calendar years are limited, taking into account the exceeded limit, such that the sum of the doses does not exceed five times the respective limit [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:9e673f6785fc2b0.text:
## § 74 Besonders zugelassene Expositionen
Under exceptional, case-by-case assessable circumstances, the competent authority may permit professional exposures deviating from § 78 Abs. 1, 2, and 4 Satz 1 of the Radiation Protection Act. For such particularly permitted exposure, the limits are:
1.  Effective dose limit: 100 mSv [StrlSchV.md].
2.  Organ-equivalent dose limit for the eye lens: 100 mSv [StrlSchV.md].
3.  Organ-equivalent dose limit for the hands, forearms, feet, and ankles: 1 Sv each [StrlSchV.md].
4.  Local skin dose limit: 1 Sv [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:1765e6b71b62ec6.text:
## § 75 Sonstige Schutzvorkehrungen
The responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that the protection of professionally exposed persons against external and internal exposure is primarily ensured by structural and technical facilities or by suitable working procedures [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:c3957b145306802.text:
## § 77 Ärztliche Überwachung beruflich exponierter Personen
For Category A persons, the responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that tasks are only performed if the person has been examined by a physician authorized according to § 175 Abs. 1 Satz 1 within one year before the initial performance of the tasks, and a certificate is available confirming no health concerns [StrlSchV.md].

wiki:summary-strlschv.md:c8e4ebbf9552594.text:
## § 85 Buchführung und Mitteilung
The responsible radiation protection officer must ensure that:
1.  The competent authority is informed within one month of the acquisition, generation, purchase, release, and other disposition of radioactive substances, stating the type and activity of the substances [StrlSchV.md].
2.  A record is kept of the acquisition, generation, purchase, release, and other disposition of radioactive substances, stating the type and activity of the substances [StrlSchV.md].
3.  The inventory of radioactive substances with half-lives greater than 100 days must be reported to the competent authority by January 31 of the following year at the end of a calendar year [StrlSchV.md].
## Documentation and Record Keeping (§ 86, § 33 Abs. 1)
The Radiation Protection Officer (*Strahlenschutzverantwortliche*) must ensure detailed record keeping regarding substances for which conformity with a release certificate has been established. This includes:
*   Bookkeeping of the substances, specifying:
    *   The determinations made according to Annexes 4 and 8, particularly the specific activity, radionuclides, dilution mass, and dilution area.
    *   The mass of the substances.
    *   The method of free measurement and the time of determination.
*   Annual reporting to the competent authority regarding:
    *   The mass of the substances.
    *   The respective type of release according to § 35, § 36, or § 37 (1).
    *   The actual remaining amount in the case of a specific release for disposal and a specific release of metal scrap for recycling.
Furthermore, records concerning substances must be retained for 30 years from the time of determination (§ 33 Abs. 1 Nr. 1) and must be deposited with the competent authority or handed over to a designated body if the activity ceases before the retention period expires [StrlSchV.md].
## Storage and Security (§ 87)
The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that:
1.  Radioactive substances whose activity exceeds the release limit of Annex 4 Table 1 Column 2, and whose specific activity exceeds the release limit of Annex 4 Table 1 Column 3, are secured against loss, misuse, and access by unauthorized persons [StrlSchV.md].
2.  Radioactive substances whose activity exceeds the release limit of Annex 4 Table 1 Column 2, and whose specific activity exceeds this limit by a hundred percent, are additionally stored in protected rooms or containment vessels until they are processed or used [StrlSchV.md].
3.  Nuclear fuels are stored such that no critical state can arise during storage [StrlSchV.md].
## Maintenance and Inspection (§ 88, § 97)
The StrlSchV mandates regular technical inspections for radiation-generating equipment:
*   **General Equipment:** Facilities for generating ionizing radiation, irradiation devices, and devices for gamma radiography must be maintained at least annually and inspected between maintenance periods by an expert designated according to § 172 (1) No. 3 of the Radiation Protection Act for technical safety, safety, and radiation protection [StrlSchV.md].
*   **X-ray Equipment:** X-ray facilities must be inspected at least every five years by an expert designated according to § 172 (1) No. 1 of the Radiation Protection Act, specifically for technical safety, safety, and radiation protection [StrlSchV.md].
*   **General Requirement:** The Radiation Protection Officer must ensure that the operating manual is kept ready for:
    *   Facilities for generating ionizing radiation.
    *   X-ray facilities.
    *   Irradiators and devices containing contained radioactive substances [StrlSchV.md].
## Environmental Monitoring and Dose Limits (§ 99, § 102, § 103)
The regulation sets specific limits for derived emissions into the environment:
*   **Derived Dose Limit:** For planning, construction, operation, decommissioning, and safe containment of nuclear technical facilities, facilities for generating ionizing radiation, and related installations, the limit for the effective dose from derived radioactive substances via air or water for individuals in the population is **0.3 millisieverts (mSv)** in a calendar year [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Alternative Monitoring:** If the competent authority deems it unnecessary to set activity amounts and concentrations, the evidence of compliance with the 0.3 mSv limit can be assumed if the concentrations according to Annex 11 Part D for derived radioactive substances via air or water do not exceed the limits in the annual average [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Monitoring:** The Radiation Protection Officer must ensure that emissions are monitored and reported to the competent authority annually, specifying the type and activity of the emissions [StrlSchV.md].
## Measurement Devices (§ 90)
The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that:
*   Appropriate radiation measuring devices are used for measuring personal dose, site dose, site dose rate, surface contamination, and the activity of air and water [StrlSchV.md].
*   For continuous measuring devices, failure must be indicated by a clearly perceptible signal, unless two or more independent measuring devices serve the same measurement purpose [StrlSchV.md].
*   Records of function testing and maintenance must be kept for ten years from the date of the test or maintenance [StrlSchV.md].
## Emergency Planning and Accident Dose Limits
The source outlines specific body dose limits to be considered when planning for accidents involving the release of radioactive substances into the environment [StrlSchV.md]:
1. An effective dose of 50 millisieverts (mSv).
2. An organ-equivalent dose of the thyroid of 150 millisievert (mSv).
3. An organ-equivalent dose of the skin, hands, forearms, feet, and ankles of each 500 millisievert (mSv).
4. An organ-equivalent dose of the eye lens, gonads, uterus, and bone marrow (red) of each 50 millisievert (mSv).
5. An organ-equivalent dose of the bone surface of 300 millisievert (mSv).
6. An organ-equivalent dose of the large intestine, lung, stomach, bladder, breast, liver, esophagus, other organs or tissues according to Annex 18 Part C Number 2 Footnote 1, where not mentioned under Number 4, of each 150 millisievert (mSv).
The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that planning for technical or structural protective measures against accidents at nuclear power plants, up to decommissioning, is carried out [StrlSchV.md]. Furthermore, planning for other facilities must consider the potential extent of damage to limit exposure in case of accidents [StrlSchV.md].
## Incident and Accident Response
*   **Preventive Measures (§ 105):** The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that systematic measures are taken when applying radioactive substances or ionizing radiation to humans to: 1. prevent an incident, 2. detect an incident, and 3. keep the adverse effects as low as possible in case of an incident [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Emergency Measures (§ 106):** The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that necessary information and consultation are provided to the authorities responsible for disaster protection and public safety for their planning to defend against dangers from ionizing radiation and to limit or eliminate adverse effects of an incident or accident [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Reporting (§ 108):** The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that the occurrence of an accident, an incident, or any other significant incident is immediately reported to the competent authority [StrlSchV.md].
## Medical Applications and Patient Protection
*   **Justification (§ 119):** The physician or dentist establishing the justifying indication must check, in addition to compliance with § 83 Abs. 3 StrlSchG, whether the planned application is a recognized procedure in medical science or a clinical trial that requires special justification by the physician or dentist [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Protection of Special Groups (§ 120):** The applying physician or dentist must inquire about pregnancy in fertile individuals. If pregnancy is present or cannot be excluded, the urgency of the application must be assessed. For open radioactive substances, the rules for breastfeeding apply [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Equipment Requirements (§ 114):** A X-ray facility used on humans must have a function that displays the parameters for determining the exposure received by the examined or treated person, or, if not possible by state-of-the-art technology, a way to determine the received exposure otherwise [StrlSchV.md].
## Quality Assurance and Record Keeping
*   **Quality Assurance (§ 115):** Before commissioning, the *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that the required quality for the application is achieved through an acceptance test by the manufacturer or supplier [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Constancy Check (§ 116):** The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that facilities are regularly checked after commissioning to ensure that the required quality is still achieved (constancy check) [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Record Keeping (§ 127):** The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that records, X-ray images, digital image data, and other examination data are stored so that they remain available and readable throughout the retention period defined by § 85 Abs. 2 StrlSchG [StrlSchV.md].
## Quality Assurance and Facilities (§ 128, § 130)
The responsible authority designates medical and dental facilities to ensure quality when applying ionizing radiation or radioactive substances to humans (§ 128). A facility can only be designated if:
1. No facts suggest concerns regarding the required independence for performing its tasks [StrlSchV.md].
2. The necessary personnel, technical, and organizational resources are available [StrlSchV.md].
3. The personnel possess the required qualifications and experience [StrlSchV.md].
4. The working methods are expected to ensure proper performance, including adherence to medical science requirements [StrlSchV.md].
5. Appropriate measures for quality assurance are available [StrlSchV.md].
The Radiation Protection Officer must ensure that the commencement and cessation of activities involving ionizing radiation or radioactive substances are immediately reported to the designated medical or dental facility [StrlSchV.md].
Medical and dental facilities are subject to quality assurance measures (§ 130). These measures specifically check whether:
1. The application is justified and medical science requirements are observed [StrlSchV.md].
2. The equipment and procedures meet the necessary quality standards according to the state of science and technology to keep exposure as low as possible [StrlSchV.md].
3. X-ray equipment and procedures meet the necessary quality standards to keep exposure as low as possible [StrlSchV.md].
4. Diagnostic reference values are not unjustifiably exceeded [StrlSchV.md].
5. A procedure exists for systematically identifying and handling occurrences during application [StrlSchV.md].
6. Written working instructions have been created according to § 121 Abs. 1 Satz 1 [StrlSchV.md].
## Medical Physics Expert Involvement (§ 131, § 132)
The Radiation Protection Officer must ensure that a Medical Physics Expert (MPE) is involved in:
*   Developing the radiation plan and conducting the treatment when radioactive substances or ionizing radiation are used based on an individual dose plan [StrlSchV.md].
*   Standardized treatments, open radioactive substance examinations, CT/3D imaging, and interventions using X-ray equipment with significant exposure [StrlSchV.md].
*   Consultation for all other applications where optimization of radiation protection or ensuring required quality is warranted [StrlSchV.md].
The MPE is responsible for:
1. Quality assurance during planning and execution, including physical-technical quality assurance [StrlSchV.md].
2. Selecting equipment, devices, and facilities [StrlSchV.md].
3. Monitoring the exposure of persons [StrlSchV.md].
4. Monitoring compliance with diagnostic reference values [StrlSchV.md].
5. Investigating occurrences [StrlSchV.md].
6. Conducting risk assessments [StrlSchV.md].
7. Instructing and training personnel [StrlSchV.md].
## Research Consent and Information Duties (§ 133 - § 136)
For research purposes, the application of radioactive substances or ionizing radiation requires consent after information and questioning (§ 133).
*   **Consent (§ 134):** The written consent must cover the application and necessary follow-up examinations. Consent can be revoked at any time [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Information (§ 135):** The leading doctor or dentist must provide written information on the nature, significance, scope, and risks of the application, and the person must be informed about the conditions and duration of the applications and the possibility of withdrawing consent [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Minors/Incapable Persons (§ 136):** Applications to persons incapable of understanding the risks, or minors, are only permitted if:
    1. The research goal cannot be achieved otherwise [StrlSchV.md].
    2. The person has a disease or suspected disease relevant to the research [StrlSchV.md].
    3. The goal is to diagnose, save life, restore health, alleviate suffering, or improve procedures related to the disease [StrlSchV.md].
    4. Legal representatives provide consent after being informed and questioned [StrlSchV.md].
## Operational Requirements and Reporting (§ 137 - § 142)
*   **Prohibitions (§ 137):** Applications are prohibited for pregnant women or institutionalized persons. The effective dose for a healthy person must not exceed 20 mSv [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Personnel (§ 145):** Applications must be performed by approved doctors or dentists, or by personnel who possess the required qualifications or knowledge and work under constant supervision [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Reporting (§ 142):** The responsible party must submit a final report to the competent supervisory authority no later than twelve months after the end of the research project, detailing the exposure for every included person [StrlSchV.md].
*   **Retention (§ 140):** Consent forms and records must be kept for 30 years after the declaration/application [StrlSchV.md].

## Measurement Devices (§ 90)
The *Strahlenschutzverantwortliche* must ensure that:

## Emergency Planning and Accident Dose Limits

1. An effective dose of 50 millisieverts (mSv).
2. An organ-equivalent dose of the thyroid of 150 millisievert (mSv).
